name: Silicon
name_origin: From the Latin 'silex' or 'silicis', meaning flint. Coined by Thomas Thomson in 1817 with the '-on' suffix to mirror carbon and boron.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Silicon is the second most abundant element in Earth's crust (~28.2% by mass) after oxygen. It is found almost exclusively combined with oxygen in silicate minerals and silica (quartz, sand).

compounds.name: Silicon dioxide (silica)
compounds.summary: Found as quartz, sand, and many crystalline forms; raw material for glass, ceramics, and an additive in foods and pharmaceuticals.

compounds.name: Silicon carbide
compounds.summary: Carborundum; an extremely hard abrasive and a wide-bandgap semiconductor used in high-power, high-temperature electronics.

compounds.name: Silicon nitride
compounds.summary: A high-strength engineering ceramic used in bearings, cutting tools, and turbine components.

compounds.name: Silane
compounds.summary: A pyrophoric gas used in the deposition of pure silicon thin films and as a doping precursor in semiconductor manufacturing.

compounds.name: Sodium silicate
compounds.summary: Water glass; used in detergents, adhesives, and as a sealant.

compounds.name: Silicones (polysiloxanes)
compounds.summary: Polymers based on Si-O backbones used in sealants, lubricants, medical implants, and cosmetics.

uses: Semiconductor wafers for integrated circuits, microprocessors, and memory chips
Silicone polymers for sealants, lubricants, and medical-grade implants
Abrasives and refractory ceramics (silicon carbide, silica brick)
reactivity_summary: Silicon is a relatively unreactive metalloid at room temperature, protected by a thin native oxide layer. At higher temperatures it reacts with halogens and with most metals to form silicides, and it dissolves in hot concentrated alkali solutions.
isotopes_summary: Silicon has three stable isotopes: Si-28 (~92.2%), Si-29 (~4.7%), and Si-30 (~3.1%). Si-32, with a half-life of about 153 years, is used to date young marine sediments.

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Silicon
